ref: As If You Were Present · 1 Corinthians 1:18; Galatians 6:14; Romans 8:17
text: Most sweet Lord Jesus,
pour into me, a sinner, I beseech You, the fullness of Your love;
that I may desire nothing earthly or carnal,
but love You alone above all things,
so that my soul may utterly refuse to be consoled,
save in You, my most sweet God.
Write with Your finger upon the tablets of my heart
the memory of all that You bore for me,
that I may have it ever before my eyes;
and grant that it may grow sweet to me not only to ponder these things,
but also, if need be, to bear them in my small measure;
and not only to serve You with all my strength,
but even, for Your sake, to suffer reproach,
or to be condemned to the basest death.
Amen.

ref: The Agony in the Garden · Luke 22:39–44 (with Matthew 26 and Mark 14)
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
who for me, a wretch, willed to be sorrowful and heavy,
grant me to bear all my sorrows unceasingly toward you;
and may you deign to bear them with me,
joined to your own sorrow.
O Jesus, who being set in prayer willed to be strengthened by an angel,
grant me, by the power of your prayer,
that your angel may always stand by me as I pray, strengthening me.

ref: Matins: The First Interrogation · John 18:19–23 (with Matthew 26, Mark 14, Luke 22)
text: O Lord Jesus, how do You sit so despised and desolate?
Where are Your disciples and Your friends?
O my one good! O my one and only joy!
What shall I do, seeing You like this?
Surely, Lord, I will sit on the ground with You,
and keep You company,
for I see no one here who loves You,
but rather Your enemies, raging and mad, who hate You.
Lord Jesus Christ, who at the morning hour, before Annas,
received a blow from a servant,
and let Yourself be led, bound, to Caiaphas,
and through the whole night bore many insults and afflictions:
grant me, a sinner, I beseech You,
that I may rejoice to suffer the like for the glory of Your name,
and may deserve to enjoy those eternal joys.
With those same eyes of Your tender mercy,
with which You then looked upon Peter,
look often upon me, wretched as I am,
who so many times denied You
by evil thoughts, words, deeds, and neglects,
that I may come to know my faults and repent of them,
and deserve to find pardon and grace with You. Amen.

ref: The Council of the Malignant · Luke 22:66–71; Matthew 27:1–4
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
who at the first hour of the day suffered yourself to be led, bound, into the council of the Jews,
and after many injuries and revilings to be led from Caiaphas to Pilate,
where you were judged by the Jews a man guilty of death and a malefactor,
and were falsely accused of many things;
and then to be sent from Pilate to Herod, and there accused and reckoned a fool,
and despised, and mocked in a white garment,
and at last, so mocked and clothed in white, sent back to Pilate:
grant me, I beseech you, to bear injuries and revilings patiently and gladly for the glory of your name,
and to appear before your face with joy,
and ever to gaze upon your desirable countenance. Amen.

ref: Terce: The Silent Lamb · Matthew 27:11–31; Mark 15:1–20; Luke 23:1–25; John 19:1–16
text: Lord Jesus Christ, who at the third hour of the day willed to be scourged for us,
free me from the scourges of that eternal wrath which I have deserved;
by the piercings of your most sacred head, pierce my mind,
that I may have strength to shun the delights that harm me,
and grant me, after penance, to attain to the crown.
You who, mocked in so many ways, were at last sentenced and condemned to death,
make me escape the devil's mockery and eternal death.
You bore your cross, O Lord: grant me to take up my cross and follow you —
my own cross, the one owed to my sins,
and my own cross willingly and devoutly borne,
that through it I may come to glory. Amen.

ref: Into Your Hands · Luke 23:46; Matthew 27:50; Mark 15:37; John 19:30
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
who at the ninth hour of the day, hanging upon the gibbet,
and crying with a loud voice, commended Your spirit into the hands of the Father,
and, with bowed head, sent forth that same spirit,
and, now dead, received in Your side the wound from the soldier's lance:
deign, I beseech You, now and always, to hold my spirit commended to You,
and to pierce my heart through with the sword of charity,
and to imprint upon it the wounds of Your body,
and by these to drive far from it all unlawful thoughts,
and at the last, at the end of my life,
to place my spirit, commended into Your hands, among the blessed spirits. Amen.

ref: The Lowering of the Body · Matthew 27; Mark 15; Luke 23; John 19
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
who at the evening hour willed to be taken down from the cross by Joseph,
and to be received between his arms;
who wast also bewailed by thy most sorrowful Mother and thy other friends:
make me, I beseech thee, a Joseph, growing daily in virtues;
and grant that, mourning over the sins for which thou wast crucified
and art again, as it were, bound upon the cross,
I may be worthy to take thee down from that cross;
and to receive thee, like a little bundle of myrrh, between the arms of my love,
where thou, my Beloved, mayest deign forever to abide,
and keep me from the snares of the devil,
that I may never, here or hereafter, be parted from thee.
Amen.

ref: The Anointing and the Tomb · John 19:38–42; Matthew 27:59–60; Mark 15:46; Luke 23:53
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
who at the hour of Compline willed to be anointed and prepared with spices,
to be wrapped and bound in the shroud and the other linens,
and, while Your Mother and Your other friends lamented,
to be carried to burial and laid in the tomb:
grant me, I pray,
that I may anoint You with spices, through fervent devotion and a good life;
that I may wrap You in shroud and linens, through pure affection and cleanness of conscience;
that I may mourn You with the tears of penitence and compassion,
and carry You in the arms of charitable and humble labor;
and that I may bury You in my heart, through unceasing remembrance and memory;
so that I too, buried together with You,
may deserve to come with You to the glory of the resurrection. Amen.

ref: The Praise of the Cross · Matthew 26:53; cf. Luke 23:1–46; Isaiah 53:5
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
who for the redemption of the world willed to bear patiently miseries and anguish,
reproaches and insults, slanders and injuries, pains and afflictions, the Passion and death:
through all these things which you endured for our sins,
free me from all sins and vices,
from all the perils of this world, from infernal punishments,
from sudden and from eternal death.
And grant to me, I beseech you,
not to flee or to forget all that you endured for me,
but to hold it always before my eyes, and to embrace it ardently;
so that, sharing in your labor and your sorrow,
you may will me also to be a partaker of your rest and your consolation.
Amen.

ref: The One Lamp Still Burning · Mark 16:1; cf. Matthew 12:40
text: O good Jesus,
the ineffable mercy of Your tenderness was not yet satisfied,
until, breaking through the bars of hell also,
You should redeem Your captives.
That blessed and most holy soul of Yours descended to the dead,
leading the bound out of darkness and the shadow of death.
Descend now also, merciful Jesus,
through this ineffable tenderness of Yours,
with Your grace and mercy
upon the souls of Your servants,
of my parents and kindred,
that You may snatch them from the pains they deserve for their sins,
and lead them to joys eternal. Amen.

ref: The Washing of the Feet · John 13:1–8
text: O Jesus, gentle example of true humility,
who didst wash the feet of thy disciples,
I ask and beseech thee, Lord, purge my affections,
that, with both feet — that is, with the twofold love —
cleansed and set aflame,
I may come secure to thee, my purifier.
Purify me unto the end of my days,
and cleanse me from every stain of sin,
that, my negligences and sins forgiven,
the enemies who watch my heel
may fall back confounded on the day of my death.
Direct, O Lord, my feet into the way of peace,
that, delivered from the hands of all my enemies,
I may bless thee with all thine elect,
unto the ages of ages. Amen.

ref: The New Day After the Sabbath · Matthew 28:1-2
text: Lord Jesus Christ, sole sweetness,
who, the chains of death being broken, glorified your body,
and rose in glory so unspeakable:
I beg and beseech you, by your flowering Resurrection,
grant that I, rising from vices and from the death of the soul,
may ever flower in virtues, and walk in newness of life;
that I may seek and savor the things that are above,
not the things that are upon the earth.
By the power of your brightness,
cleanse my soul from the darkness of sins;
and by that same power, on the day of the universal resurrection,
let my flesh rise again to glory,
that with you I may rejoice eternally in both. Amen.

ref: The First to See Him Risen · Cf. John 21:25; Mark 16:9 (Ludolf's pious tradition of the Resurrection)
text: O Mary, Mother of God and gracious Virgin,
true consoler of all the desolate who cry out to you:
by that great joy with which you were consoled,
when you knew the Lord Jesus had risen on the third day from the dead, beyond all suffering,
be the consoler of my soul;
and before that same your Son, the only-begotten of God,
on the last day, when I shall rise again with soul and body,
and must render an account of each of my deeds,
deign to help me,
that through you, holy Mother and Virgin,
I may escape the sentence of everlasting damnation,
and with all God's chosen come happily to joys eternal. Amen.

ref: All Hail · Matthew 28:9–10; John 20:18
text: O Jesus, our redemption, our love and our longing,
who to the devout women seeking You, desiring You,
and returning from Your tomb,
rising from the dead in mercy willed to come and appear —
I seek You, whom my soul loves, O sweetest Jesus;
I seek, and I do not find:
come, then, to my soul that seeks You,
O only Son of God the Father.
And if You delay Your desirable presence now,
do not take it from me in the time to come;
that in the last resurrection of the faithful
You may show Yourself to me, gentle and kind, with them,
and that of Your presence I may forever rejoice with Your elect. Amen.

ref: The Stranger on the Road · Luke 24:13–27
text: Lord Jesus, faithful friend, faithful companion, kind Lord,
you could not bear to see your own so grieved and so astray.
When I am weighed down by perplexity and weariness,
and speak of you devoutly upon the road,
be at once present to me.
Strengthen my heart and enlighten it,
and set it aflame with love of you.
Journey with me, and do not only journey,
but teach me, opening the Scriptures of yourself,
that my eyes, now held by doubt, may know you.
For you do not forget those who are mindful of you;
and those who remember your Passion you accompany the more closely.
Be in the midst of us, as you promised,
where two or three are gathered in your name. Amen.

ref: The Wound and the Word · John 20:24–29
text: My Lord and my God.
Thou art my Lord, who redeemed me with Thy blood and made me Thine own;
Thou art my God, who created me and formed me from the slime of the earth.
Now I doubt nothing; I am certain.
I confirm Thy Resurrection; I proclaim Thy immortality.
Thou art my Lord and my Master; I am Thy servant, and the son of Thy handmaid.
Let me put my hand into that sweetest side, that most healing side —
the side whence we are saved from wrath,
whence we are healed of our guilt,
whence we are reborn into grace,
whence we are lifted up into glory.
This is my faith: thus I feel, and thus I believe.
And though I have not seen Thee in the flesh, make me blessed in believing. Amen.

ref: The Fire That Does Not Consume · Acts 2:1–4
text: O Holy Spirit, the Spirit of truth,
come now and descend, fill us and console us,
strengthen us and instruct us,
and bestow upon us the fullness of virtues and of joys.
Purge us by holy fear,
melt our hardness by piety,
make firm our weakness by fortitude,
enlighten our darkness by understanding,
and with the inextinguishable ardor of charity kindle us to all love.
Consume in us whatever is worthy to be consumed,
and when it is consumed, be in us no longer a fire that consumes, but one that enlightens.
Give light to the just, and to this wretched sinner give it also;
carry us out of ourselves, and draw us away from the affections of the mind,
and bear off from us the eternal damnation we have deserved.

ref: The Master Asleep in the Boat · Matthew 8:23–27
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
command the blasts and motions of suggestions and temptations;
come, and walk upon the waves of my heart,
that all within me may become serene and tranquil.
Let my heart rest in You, my God;
let my heart, a great sea swelling with waves,
sleep to all things that are beneath heaven,
and keep watch in You,
that I may embrace You, my one good,
and contemplate You, the light of my eyes,
and rejoicing may sing and say:
I sleep, and my heart keeps watch;
and that other word: In peace, in the selfsame, I will sleep and take my rest.
Amen.

ref: On the Waves of the Night · Matthew 14:24–31 (with Mark 6, John 6)
text: Lord Jesus Christ, most merciful,
deign to climb into the little boat of my heart,
and still the windy gusts of pride and the storms of rising vices,
that no wind of temptation may overturn me, nor any wave drown me.
Give me counsel in disturbance,
help in persecution,
consolation in tribulation,
fortitude in adversity,
and strength in every temptation.
Free me from the tempests of this stormy sea,
and lead me to the tranquillity of the quiet shore,
granting me now the peace of time and of heart,
and after this, the peace of eternity. Amen.

ref: Our Father · Matthew 6:9
text: O Power, O Word of wondrous working!
Come now, my good Lord Jesus, Word of the Father,
complete Thou my prayer,
perfect Thou these words spoken by Thee and laid upon my mouth.
Perfect them, and say Amen; say, let it be done;
say to me as Thou saidst to that Syrophoenician woman:
Be it done to thee as thou wilt.
O sweet love of Jesus, O sweet truth,
O sweet Amen, O sweet word, 'let it be':
Be it done to me according to Thy word.
Let it be done. Amen.

ref: The King Enrolled Among Us · Luke 2:1–5
text: Would that You, Lord Jesus,
would make me, a wretched one, to be among Your chosen,
that You might enroll me in eternity.
To You, our King, let me profess the tribute of faith and of justice,
in heart, in mouth, and in deed.

ref: The Word Made Flesh in Her · Luke 1:26–34
text: O Jesus, Son of the living God,
who by the will of the Father, the Holy Spirit working with Him,
came forth from that same Father's bosom
like a river flowing from a place of delight,
bending down through the lowly valleys,
looking upon the lowliness of Thy handmaid,
and didst descend into the womb of the Virgin,
where, conceived ineffably, Thou didst take flesh:
let Thy grace descend, O merciful Jesus, eagerly,
through the merits of the Mother and Virgin,
upon me, Thy most unworthy servant,
that through her I may desire Thee,
and within, in love, may conceive Thee;
and through that same grace working in me,
may bring forth the saving fruits of good works. Amen.

ref: Upon the Mountain · Matthew 5:1–5
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
who, that You might teach the higher summits of the virtues,
ascended the mountain with Your disciples,
and there taught the blessednesses and the sublime virtues,
and promised to each its fitting reward:
grant to me, frail as I am,
that hearing Your voice,
I may strive through the exercise of the virtues to have merit,
so that, in Your mercy, I may obtain also the reward.
Make me, who consider the reward, not refuse the work of the reward,
but let the hope of eternal salvation soften in me the pain of the present remedy,
and kindle my soul to the brightness of the work.
Make me, wretched now, blessed —
blessed with the blessedness of the way through grace,
and at the last blessed with the blessedness of the homeland through glory.
Amen.

ref: The Star and the Three Kings · Matthew 2:1–2
text: O good Jesus, born of the Virgin,
who revealed Yourself to the Magi with a star to lead them,
and made them return to their own country by another way:
merciful Jesus, let the light of Your grace shine upon the darkness of my conscience,
and by Your joyful appearing, give me full knowledge of You and of myself,
that within I may see You, within I may find You;
and there offer to Your majesty the myrrh of inward compunction,
the incense of devout prayer,
the gold of holy love;
and that I, who by the way of darkness and of guilt withdrew
from the homeland of supernal happiness,
may return to it by the way of truth and grace, with You to lead me. Amen.

ref: The Boy Lost and Found · Luke 2:40–46
text: Lord Jesus Christ, Son of the living God,
who for three days were sought by your grieving parents,
and at last were found in the Temple:
grant to me, a wretch, to desire you,
by desiring to seek you,
by seeking to find you,
by finding to love you,
by loving to redeem my evils,
and, once redeemed, to do them no more.
You who give freely to him that asks,
are found by him that seeks,
are opened to him that knocks:
deny not to me, the least, what you promise to all.
And you who, returning to Nazareth at your parents' will,
and made subject to them, showed the pattern of obedience:
grant to me, hardened, to break my own will,
that I may be subject to you,
and, for your sake, to every human creature. Amen.

ref: The Desert of Forty Days · Matthew 4:1–2; Luke 4:1–2
text: Lord Jesus, You went into the desert not for Your own need, but for ours.
Lead me by Your Holy Spirit, and not by any feigning of the evil one,
into the desert of my own heart and of quiet contemplation,
that, resting from the noise of the world, I may better wait upon God.
Teach me to crucify my flesh with its desires,
and, leaving the world behind in my mind,
to hunger for the eternal joys alone, as for the manna of the desert.
Prepare my soul for temptation, and gird me, in the bright days and the dark nights alike,
against the enemy who never ceases to assail me.
Amen.

ref: Lazarus, Our Friend, Sleeps · John 11:1–17
text: Lord Jesus, I behold Thy wondrous goodness toward Thine own.
I do not say, Come and heal; I dare not command Thee.
I say only: behold, he whom Thou lovest is sick.
It is enough that Thou knowest, for Thou dost not love and abandon.
Thou who art the consoler of the grieving, the healer of the languishing,
if Thou dost scourge me, break me unto salvation,
and let me not remain whole unto damnation.
Call my death a sleep, for the hope of resurrection;
and wake me, when Thou wilt, out of sleep.
Strengthen me in faith, that seeing, I may more firmly believe in Thee.

ref: The Water Made Wine · John 2:1–4
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
who on the third day — that is, in the time of grace —
came in the flesh to the wedding,
the joining of Thyself and Thy Church,
and didst turn water into wine,
showing that the things which in the old Scripture seemed carnal are spiritual:
Turn, I beseech Thee, my cold soul into the fervor of Thy love;
my tasteless soul, into the savor of Thy sweetness;
my unsteady, wavering soul, into the constancy of Thy strength and grace.
Change the water of all my snow-cold lukewarmness
into the wine of fruitful compunction,
that I, wretched as I am, may drink of it and be made drunk,
and at the last, of Thy mercy, vouchsafe to turn it into the wine of gladness. Amen.

ref: The Widow of Nain · Luke 7:11–16
text: Lord Jesus Christ, come into Nain, into my soul tossed by temptations;
draw near to the gate, holding back the senses, that they may not pass into the affections;
come by grace, touch the heart by correction;
let the bearers — the occasions and opportunities of sinning — stand still;
say to the soul lying in its sins that it may sit up by good will,
begin to speak by confession,
and arise by good works;
give it back to its mother, the nurse of grace, that it may stand fast in steadfastness;
and so may Your truth visit Your people — the powers of my soul, its affections and thoughts — by knowledge,
Your power by working, and Your goodness by keeping me. Amen.
